11126.4.5.summary_2:
The Tribal Nation Grant Panel may hold a closed session to discuss confidential information related to the administration of Article 2.3 (starting Section 12019.30) and the internal affairs of an eligible tribe, including finances and competitive business plans.
- Closed-session discussion is limited strictly to information pertinent to the agendized item and may not cover other topics.
- Before entering closed session, the Panel must publicly announce the type of information to be discussed, and this announcement must be recorded in the minutes.
- Any action on items discussed under this section must be taken in open session. For definitional purposes, "Tribal Nation Grant Panel" and "eligible tribe" have the same meanings as in Article 2.3.

11126.5.text: In the event that any meeting is willfully interrupted by a group or groups of persons so as to render the orderly conduct of such meeting unfeasible and order cannot be restored by the removal of individuals who are willfully interrupting the meeting the state body conducting the meeting may order the meeting room cleared and continue in session. Nothing in this section shall prohibit the state body from establishing a procedure for readmitting an individual or individuals not responsible for willfully disturbing the orderly conduct of the meeting. Notwithstanding any other provision of law, only matters appearing on the agenda may be considered in such a session. Representatives of the press or other news media, except those participating in the disturbance, shall be allowed to attend any session held pursuant to this section.
11126.5.summary_1: If a meeting is willfully disrupted to the point that orderly conduct cannot be restored, the state body may order the meeting room cleared and continue in session. Readmission may be allowed for individuals not responsible for the disturbance, and only items on the agenda may be considered during the continued session. Press representatives may attend the session, except those participating in the disturbance.
11126.5.summary_2:
The state body may order the meeting room cleared and continue the session if a group willfully disrupts proceedings to the point that orderly conduct cannot be restored by removing the interrupters, and it may establish a procedure to readmit individuals not responsible for the disturbance.
In such a session, only matters on the agenda may be considered, and representatives of the press or other news media may attend unless they participated in the disturbance.

11126.7.text: No fees may be charged by a state body for providing a notice required by Section 11125 or for carrying out any provision of this article, except as specifically authorized pursuant to this article.
11126.7.summary_1: Prohibits state bodies from charging fees for providing notices required by Section 11125 or for carrying out any provision of this article, except where fees are expressly authorized by the article.
11126.7.summary_2:
State bodies may not charge fees for providing notices required by 11125 or for carrying out any provision of this article. 
Any fee allowed must be expressly authorized by this article. 
This prohibition ensures free access to notices and related actions unless the article itself authorizes fees.

11127.text: Each provision of this article shall apply to every state body unless the body is specifically excepted from that provision by law or is covered by any other conflicting provision of law.
11127.summary_1: All provisions of the article apply to every state body unless explicitly exempted by law or overridden by another conflicting provision of law.
11127.summary_2: Broadly, every state body is subject to the provisions of this article. Exceptions exist only when a law expressly exempts the body or when another provision of law conflicts; in the latter case, the conflicting law governs.

11128.text: Each closed session of a state body shall be held only during a regular or special meeting of the body.
11128.summary_1: Closed sessions may only occur during a regular or special meeting of the state body. This restriction ensures that any deliberations conducted in private happen within the framework of a scheduled meeting rather than outside of it.
11128.summary_2: A state body may conduct its closed sessions only during a regular or special meeting, prohibiting any closed-session activity outside those meetings.

11128.5.annotation: Added by Stats. 1997, Ch. 949, Sec. 11.   Effective January 1, 1998.
11128.5.text: The state body may adjourn any regular, adjourned regular, special, or adjourned special meeting to a time and place specified in the order of adjournment. Less than a quorum may so adjourn from time to time. If all members are absent from any regular or adjourned regular meeting, the clerk or secretary of the state body may declare the meeting adjourned to a stated time and place and he or she shall cause a written notice of the adjournment to be given in the same manner as provided in Section 11125.4 for special meetings, unless that notice is waived as provided for special meetings. A copy of the order or notice of adjournment shall be conspicuously posted on or near the door of the place where the regular, adjourned regular, special, or adjourned special meeting was held within 24 hours after the time of the adjournment. When a regular or adjourned regular meeting is adjourned as provided in this section, the resulting adjourned regular meeting is a regular meeting for all purposes. When an order of adjournment of any meeting fails to state the hour at which the adjourned meeting is to be held, it shall be held at the hour specified for regular meetings by law or regulation.
11128.5.summary_1: Allows a state body to adjourn any regular, adjourned regular, special, or adjourned special meeting to a specified time and place, including adjournments with less than a quorum. If all members are absent, the clerk may declare the adjournment and must issue written notice as for special meetings; a copy of the adjournment order or notice must be posted at the meeting location within 24 hours. The adjourned regular meeting is treated as a regular meeting for all purposes, and if the adjournment order does not specify a time, the meeting is held at the hour prescribed for regular meetings by law or regulation.
11128.5.summary_2:
The state body may adjourn any regular, adjourned regular, special, or adjourned special meeting to a time and place specified in the order of adjournment. A less-than-quorum condition permits adjournment as needed.
If all members are absent from any regular or adjourned regular meeting, the clerk or secretary may declare the meeting adjourned to a stated time and place and must issue written notice of the adjournment in the same manner as for special meetings under Section 11125.4, unless such notice is waived as allowed for special meetings.
A copy of the order or notice of adjournment must be conspicuously posted at or near the door of the meeting location within 24 hours after adjournment.
If a regular or adjourned regular meeting is adjourned under this section, the resulting adjourned regular meeting is considered a regular meeting for all purposes. If the adjournment order does not specify the adjourned hour, the meeting must be held at the hour designated for regular meetings by law or regulation.

11129.annotation: Amended by Stats. 1997, Ch. 949, Sec. 12.   Effective January 1, 1998.
11129.text: Any hearing being held, or noticed or ordered to be held by a state body at any meeting may by order or notice of continuance be continued or recontinued to any subsequent meeting of the state body in the same manner and to the same extent set forth in Section 11128.5 for the adjournment of meetings. A copy of the order or notice of continuance shall be conspicuously posted on or near the door of the place where the hearing was held within 24 hours after the time of the continuance; provided, that if the hearing is continued to a time less than 24 hours after the time specified in the order or notice of hearing, a copy of the order or notice of continuance of hearing shall be posted immediately following the meeting at which the order or declaration of continuance was adopted or made.
11129.summary_1: It allows a hearing to be continued or recontinued to a later meeting by order or notice, in the same manner as adjournments under Section 11128.5. A copy of the continuance order or notice must be conspicuously posted at the hearing location within 24 hours after the continuance. If the continuation occurs less than 24 hours from the original time, posting must be done immediately after the meeting at which the continuance was adopted.
11129.summary_2:
Continuances of hearings held or noticed by a state body may be effected by an order or notice of continuance and moved to a subsequent meeting in the same manner and to the same extent as adjournments under 11128.5.
A copy of the continuance order or notice must be conspicuously posted at or near the door of the hearing location within 24 hours after the continuance; if the continuance is to a time less than 24 hours after the time specified, posting must occur immediately after the meeting at which the continuance was adopted or declared.

11130.annotation: Amended by Stats. 2009, Ch. 88, Sec. 43.   (AB 176)   Effective January 1, 2010.
11130.text:
(a) The Attorney General, the district attorney, or any interested person may commence an action by mandamus, injunction, or declaratory relief for the purpose of stopping or preventing violations or threatened violations of this article or to determine the applicability of this article to past actions or threatened future action by members of the state body or to determine whether any rule or action by the state body to penalize or otherwise discourage the expression of one or more of its members is valid or invalid under the laws of this state or of the United States, or to compel the state body to audio record its closed sessions as hereinafter provided.
(b) The court in its discretion may, upon a judgment of a violation of Section 11126, order the state body to audio record its closed sessions and preserve the audio recordings for the period and under the terms of security and confidentiality the court deems appropriate.
(c) (1) Each recording so kept shall be immediately labeled with the date of the closed session recorded and the title of the clerk or other officer who shall be custodian of the recording.
    (2) The audio recordings shall be subject to the following discovery procedures:
        (A) In any case in which discovery or disclosure of the audio recording is sought by the Attorney General, the district attorney, or the plaintiff in a civil action pursuant to this section or Section 11130.3 alleging that a violation of this article has occurred in a closed session that has been recorded pursuant to this section, the party seeking discovery or disclosure shall file a written notice of motion with the appropriate court with notice to the governmental agency that has custody and control of the audio recording. The notice shall be given pursuant to subdivision (b) of Section 1005 of the Code of Civil Procedure.
        (B) The notice shall include, in addition to the items required by Section 1010 of the Code of Civil Procedure, all of the following:
                (i) Identification of the proceeding in which discovery or disclosure is sought, the party seeking discovery or disclosure, the date and time of the meeting recorded, and the governmental agency that has custody and control of the recording.
                (ii) An affidavit that contains specific facts indicating that a violation of the act occurred in the closed session.
    (3) If the court, following a review of the motion, finds that there is good cause to believe that a violation has occurred, the court may review, in camera, the recording of that portion of the closed session alleged to have violated the act.
    (4) If, following the in camera review, the court concludes that disclosure of a portion of the recording would be likely to materially assist in the resolution of the litigation alleging violation of this article, the court shall, in its discretion, make a certified transcript of the portion of the recording a public exhibit in the proceeding.
    (5) Nothing in this section shall permit discovery of communications that are protected by the attorney-client privilege.
11130.summary_1: Authorizes actions by the Attorney General, district attorneys, or any interested person to stop or prevent violations of the act and to resolve questions about its applicability to past or threatened actions, including challenges to penalties on expression. It empowers the court to order a state body to audio-record its closed sessions and to preserve the recordings under security and confidentiality. It sets labeling requirements for recordings and prescribes discovery procedures, including in-camera review and the option for a public transcript as a court exhibit, while protecting attorney-client communications from disclosure.
11130.summary_2:
Authorized plaintiffs, including the Attorney General, district attorney, or any interested person, may seek mandamus, injunction, or declaratory relief to stop or prevent violations or threatened violations of this article, to determine the applicability of the article to past or threatened future actions by a state body, to assess whether any rule or action penalizing or discouraging expression by members is valid under state or United States law, and to compel a state body to audio record its closed sessions as provided.
If a court finds a violation of Section 11126, it may order the state body to audio record its closed sessions and preserve the recordings for the period and under security and confidentiality terms the court deems appropriate.
Each recording kept under this provision must be labeled immediately with the date of the closed session and the custodian's title. Discovery of these recordings is governed as follows: the Attorney General, district attorney, or plaintiff may file a written notice of motion with the court and must notify the custodian; the notice must identify the proceeding, the party, the date and time of the meeting, and the custodian, and must include an affidavit with specific facts indicating a violation occurred in the closed session.
The court may, after in-camera review of the relevant portion, determine that disclosure would materially assist the resolution of the litigation and may order a certified transcript of that portion to be made a public exhibit. However, discovery may not reveal communications protected by the attorney-client privilege.

11130.3.annotation: Amended by Stats. 1999, Ch. 393, Sec. 5.   Effective January 1, 2000.
11130.3.text:
(a) Any interested person may commence an action by mandamus, injunction, or declaratory relief for the purpose of obtaining a judicial determination that an action taken by a state body in violation of Section 11123 or 11125 is null and void under this section. Any action seeking such a judicial determination shall be commenced within 90 days from the date the action was taken. Nothing in this section shall be construed to prevent a state body from curing or correcting an action challenged pursuant to this section.
(b) An action shall not be determined to be null and void if any of the following conditions exist:
    (1) The action taken was in connection with the sale or issuance of notes, bonds, or other evidences of indebtedness or any contract, instrument, or agreement related thereto.
    (2) The action taken gave rise to a contractual obligation upon which a party has, in good faith, detrimentally relied.
    (3) The action taken was in substantial compliance with Sections 11123 and 11125.
    (4) The action taken was in connection with the collection of any tax.
11130.3.summary_1: Authorizes any interested person to bring mandamus, injunction, or declaratory relief to have a state body action that violates open meetings requirements (11123 or 11125) declared null and void within 90 days. However, the action may not void the act if it involves the sale or issuance of notes, bonds, or other evidences of indebtedness or related contracts; if a party detrimentally relied on a contractual obligation in good faith; if the action was in substantial compliance with 11123 and 11125; or if it concerns the collection of taxes. The statute also permits the state body to cure or correct the challenged action.
11130.3.summary_2:
This section authorizes any interested person to file a mandamus, injunction, or declaratory relief action to obtain a judicial determination that a state body's action taken in violation of Sections 11123 or 11125 is null and void under this section. The action must be commenced within 90 days from the date the action was taken, and nothing here prevents a state body from curing or correcting the challenged action.
An action will not be deemed null and void if any of the following conditions apply: (1) the action involved the sale or issuance of notes, bonds, or other evidences of indebtedness or related contracts or instruments; (2) the action gave rise to a contractual obligation that a party has in good faith detrimentally relied upon; (3) the action was in substantial compliance with Sections 11123 and 11125; or (4) the action related to the collection of any tax.
These carve-outs limit the effectiveness of nullity challenges in financial or contractual contexts, reflect reliance on contractual obligations, acknowledge substantial compliance, and address tax-collection actions. Practitioners should assess whether any of these exceptions apply to avoid a successful nullity claim.

11130.5.text:
A court may award court costs and reasonable attorney's fees to the plaintiff in an action brought pursuant to Section 11130 or 11130.3 where it is found that a state body has violated the provisions of this article. The costs and fees shall be paid by the state body and shall not become a personal liability of any public officer or employee thereof.
A court may award court costs and reasonable attorney's fees to a defendant in any action brought pursuant to Section 11130 or 11130.3 where the defendant has prevailed in a final determination of the action and the court finds that the action was clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit.
11130.5.summary_1: Authorizes courts to award costs and reasonable attorney's fees in actions brought to enforce this article when a state body is found to have violated its provisions, with the costs paid by the state body rather than by individual officers. It also allows the prevailing defendant to recover costs and fees if the court determines the action was frivolous and totally lacking in merit. This mechanism promotes accountability and deters frivolous litigation against public bodies.
11130.5.summary_2:
If a state body is found to have violated the provisions of this article in an action brought under Section 11130 or 11130.3, a court may award the plaintiff court costs and reasonable attorney's fees. These costs and fees are to be paid by the state body, and they do not become a personal liability of any public officer or employee.
The award to the plaintiff is not automatic; it rests in the court's discretion to determine whether a violation occurred and to grant the applicable costs and fees accordingly.
Conversely, if the defendant prevailed in a final determination of an action under Section 11130 or 11130.3, and the court finds that the action was clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit, the court may award the defendant court costs and reasonable attorney's fees.
The standard for awarding fees to a defendant--"clearly frivolous and totally lacking in merit"--sets a high bar, ensuring that only meriting punishment for frivolous pursuits triggers costs against the plaintiff.

11130.7.annotation: Amended by Stats. 1997, Ch. 949, Sec. 14.   Effective January 1, 1998.
11130.7.text: Each member of a state body who attends a meeting of that body in violation of any provision of this article, and where the member intends to deprive the public of information to which the member knows or has reason to know the public is entitled under this article, is guilty of a misdemeanor.
11130.7.summary_1: Imposes a misdemeanor penalty on any state-body member who attends a meeting in violation of the article with the intent to deprive the public of information the public is entitled to. The provision reinforces accountability for noncompliance with open meetings requirements.
11130.7.summary_2: Any state body member who attends a meeting in violation of this article and, knowing or having reason to know that the public is entitled to the information, intends to deprive the public of that information commits a misdemeanor.

11131.annotation: Amended by Stats. 2007, Ch. 568, Sec. 32.   Effective January 1, 2008.
11131.text: No state agency shall conduct any meeting, conference, or other function in any facility that prohibits the admittance of any person, or persons, on the basis of ancestry or any characteristic listed or defined in Section 11135, or that is inaccessible to disabled persons, or where members of the public may not be present without making a payment or purchase. As used in this section, "state agency" means and includes every state body, office, officer, department, division, bureau, board, council, commission, or other state agency.
11131.summary_1: It prohibits state agencies from holding meetings in facilities that deny admittance based on ancestry or other protected characteristics, that are inaccessible to disabled persons, or that require a payment for attendance. It also clarifies that 'state agency' includes every state body, office, officer, department, division, bureau, board, council, commission, or other state agency. These provisions promote inclusive, open access to public meetings by ensuring venues are accessible and non-discriminatory.
11131.summary_2:
The section prohibits any state agency from holding a meeting, conference, or other function in a facility that (a) excludes admittance based on ancestry or protected characteristics defined in Section 11135, (b) is inaccessible to disabled persons, or (c) restricts public attendance by requiring payment or a purchase. The prohibition applies to all such events, regardless of format or setting.
For purposes of this provision, "state agency" includes every state body, office, officer, department, division, bureau, board, council, commission, or other state agency.

11131.5.annotation: Added by Stats. 1997, Ch. 949, Sec. 16.   Effective January 1, 1998.
11131.5.text: No notice, agenda, announcement, or report required under this article need identify any victim or alleged victim of crime, tortious sexual conduct, or child abuse unless the identity of the person has been publicly disclosed.
11131.5.summary_1: Section 11131.5 restricts disclosure by prohibiting identifying victims or alleged victims of crime, tortious sexual conduct, or child abuse in notices, agendas, announcements, or reports under this article, unless the identity has already been publicly disclosed. The rule emphasizes privacy and restricts public records to prevent unnecessary exposure of victim identity. It thereby limits what must be disclosed in public proceedings to protect individuals' safety and privacy.
11131.5.summary_2: It prohibits identifying victims or alleged victims of crime, tortious sexual conduct, or child abuse in notices, agendas, announcements, or reports required under this article unless the individual's identity has already been publicly disclosed.

11132.text: Except as expressly authorized by this article, no closed session may be held by any state body.
11132.summary_1: State bodies must conduct proceedings in open sessions, with closed sessions prohibited except as expressly authorized by the article. This tightens transparency by limiting private deliberations to authorized circumstances.
11132.summary_2: Closed sessions are prohibited for all state bodies unless the article expressly authorizes them; only those authorized provisions may permit a nonpublic meeting, and state bodies must adhere to the article's express authorization when considering or conducting any closed session.
